Name: pacbayes_jacobian_skipping
Title: PAC-Bayes Guided Jacobian-Based Layer-Skipping for Training-Free Inference Optimization
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, we can develop a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism that optimizes inference latency while maintaining model accuracy, without requiring additional training.
Related Work: Existing work on training-free optimization, such as HiLoRA and EMQ, focuses on domain generalization and mixed-precision quantization. Our proposal distinguishes itself by integrating PAC-Bayes theory with Jacobian-based layer-skipping to directly optimize inference latency, a direction not yet explored in the literature.
Abstract: Efficient inference in machine learning models is crucial for real-time applications. Traditional approaches often require additional training or heuristic-based methods, which can be suboptimal and computationally expensive. We propose a novel training-free inference optimization framework that leverages PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to guide Jacobian-based layer-skipping. Our approach dynamically skips layers based on their contribution to the final output, ensuring minimal latency while maintaining model accuracy. We validate our method on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating significant improvements in inference speed without compromising accuracy.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate the effectiveness of PAC-Bayes guided Jacobian-based layer-skipping on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets. Metrics: Inference latency, accuracy.
Experiment 2: Compare our method against state-of-the-art training-free optimization techniques (e.g., HiLoRA, EMQ). Metrics: Inference speedup, accuracy drop.
Experiment 3: Analyze the impact of spectral stability bounds on layer-skipping decisions. Metrics: Stability radius, accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: The proposed method may not generalize well to all model architectures.
Risk 2: The computational overhead of calculating Jacobian matrices could offset latency gains.
Limitation 1: The method is currently limited to models where layer contributions can be effectively approximated by Jacobians.
